volumeTitle: Casina. The Casket Comedy. Curculio. Epidicus. The Two Menaechmuses
label: Casina (Section prologue.pr.1 - Section 2.2.186)

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.1
sections.original: Salvere iubeo spectatores optumos ,
sections.translation: I bid you , most worthy Spectators , welcome ; who most highly esteem the Goddess Faith [*] , and Faith esteems you . If I have said the truth , then give me loud applause , that even now , from the very beginning forward , I may know that you are favourably disposed towards me .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.5
sections.original: qui utuntur vino vetere sapientis puto
sections.translation: Those who make use of aged wine , I deem to be wise ; and those as well , who , through choice , are the spectators of ancient Plays . Since antique works and words are pleasing to you , ’ tis just that ancient Plays should in preference please you ; for the new Comedies which come out now-a-days

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.10
sections.original: multo sunt nequiores quam nummi novi .
sections.translation: are much more worthless than the new-coined money [*] . We , since we have heard the report in public , that you ardently wish for the Plays of Plautus , have brought forward this ancient Comedy of his , which you , who are among the older ones , have formerly approved .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.15
sections.original: nam iuniorum qui sunt non norunt , scio ;
sections.translation: But I am aware that those who are among the younger ones are not acquainted with it ; still , that they may make acquaintance with it , we will carefully use our best endeavours . When this was first represented , it surpassed all other Plays . In those days there was the very elite of the poets , who have now departed hence to the place common to all .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.20
sections.original: sed tamen absentes prosunt pro praesentibus .
sections.translation: But though departed , yet do they prove of advantage to those who are still existing . All of you , with the greatest earnestness , I would have entreated that you ’ ll kindly lend attention to this our company . Dismiss from your thoughts cares and monies due ; let no man stand in dread of his duns .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.25
sections.original: ludi sunt , ludus datus est argentariis ;
sections.translation: ’ Tis a holiday this — to the bankers a holiday has been given . ’ Tis now a calm ; about the Forum these are Halcyon days [*] . Reasonably do they act : during the games [*] they ask no man for money ; but during the games to no one do they pay . If your ears are disengaged , give me your attention ;

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.30
sections.original: comoediai nomen dare vobis volo .
sections.translation: I wish to mention to you the name of the Play . Clerumenae [*] this Comedy is called in Greek ; in Latin , Sortientes . Diphilus wrote it in Greek , and after that , over again , Plautus with the barking name [*] in Latin afresh .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.35
sections.original: senex híc maritus habitat ; éi est filius ,
sections.translation: An old married man is living here ; he has a son ; he , with his father , is dwelling in this house . He has a certain slave , who with disease is confined — aye , faith , to his bed , he really is , that I may tell no lie . But sixteen years ago , it happened that on a time this servant ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.40
sections.original: quom conspicatust primulo crepusculo
sections.translation: at early dawn , beheld a female child being exposed . He went at once to the woman who was exposing it , and begged her to give it to himself . He gained his request : he took it away , and carried it straight home . He gave it to his mistress , and entreated her to take care of it , and bring it up .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.45
sections.original: era fecit , educavit magna industria ,
sections.translation: His mistress did so ; with great care she brought it up , as though it had been her own daughter , not much different . Since then she has grown up to that age to be able to prove an attraction to the men ; but this old gentleman loves this girl distractedly , and , on the other hand , so does his son as well .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.50
sections.original: nunc sibi utérque contra legiones parat ,
sections.translation: Each of them now , on either side , is preparing his legions , both father and son , each unknown to the other . The father has deputed his bailiff to ask her as his wife ; he hopes that , if she ’ s given to him , an attraction out of doors will be , unknown to his wife , provided for him .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.55
sections.original: filius is autem armigerum adlegavit suom ,
sections.translation: But the son has deputed his armour-bearer to ask her for himself as a wife . He knows that if he gains that request , there will be an object for him to love , within his abode . The wife of the old gentleman has found out that he is gratifying his amorousness ; for that reason , she is making common cause together with her son .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.60
sections.original: ille autem postquam filium sensit suom
sections.translation: But this father , when he found out that his son was in love with this same woman , and was a hindrance to him , sent the young man hence upon business abroad . His mother , understanding this , still lends him , though absent , her assistance . Don ’ t you expect it ; he will not , in this Play , to-day ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.65
sections.original: in urbem non redibit : Plautus noluit ,
sections.translation: return to the city . Plautus did not choose it : he broke down the bridge that lay before him in the way . There are some here , who , I fancy , are now saying among themselves , Prithee , what means this , i ’ faith ?— the marriage of a slave [*] Are slaves to be marrying wives , or asking them for themselves ?

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.70
sections.original: novom attulerunt , quod fit nusquam gentium .”
sections.translation: They ’ ve introduced something new — a thing that ’ s done nowhere in the world . But I affirm that this is done in Greece [*] , and at Carthage , and here in our own country , and in the Apulian country ; and that the marriages of slaves are wont to be solemnized there with more fuss than even those of free persons .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.75
sections.original: id ni fit , mecum pignus si quis volt dato
sections.translation: If this is not the fact , if any one pleases , let him bet with me a stake towards a jug of honied wine [*] , so long as a Carthaginian is the umpire in my cause , or a Greek in fact , or an Apulian . ( A pause .) What now ? You don ’ t take it ? No one ’ s thirsty , I find . I ’ ll return to that foundling girl ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.80
sections.original: quam servi summa vi sibi uxorem expetunt ,
sections.translation: whom the two slaves are , with all their might , contending for as a wife . She ’ ll be found to be both chaste and free , of freeborn parents , an Athenian girl , and assuredly of no immodesty at all will she be guilty [*] in this Comedy at least . But i ’ faith , for sure , directly afterwards , when the Play is over ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.85
sections.original: argentum si quis dederit , ut ego suspicor ,
sections.translation: if any one offers the money , as I guess , she ’ ll readily enter into matrimony with him , and not wait for good omens . Thus much I have to say . Farewell ; be prosperous in your affairs , and conquer by true valour , as hitherto you ’ ve done [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.1.89
sections.original: Non mihi licere meam rem me solum , ut volo ,
sections.translation: Isn ’ t it to be allowed me for myself to speak and think about my own affairs by myself , just as I choose , without you as an overlooker ? Why the plague are you following me about ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.91b
sections.original: Quia certum est mihi ,
sections.translation: Because I ’ m resolved , just like your shadow , wherever you go , to follow you about . Why troth , even if you are ready to go to the cross , I ’ m determined to follow you . Hence judge of the sequel ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.95
sections.original: possisne necne clam me sutelis tuis
sections.translation: whether you can or not , by your artifices , slily deprive me of Casina for a wife , just as you are attempting .

sections.label: Section 1.1.97
sections.original: Quid tibi negotist mecum ?
sections.translation: What business have you with me ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.97b
sections.original: Quid ais , impudens ?
sections.translation: What say you , impudence ? Why are you creeping about in the city , you bailiff [*] , so very valuable in this place ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.99
sections.original: Lubet .
sections.translation: Because I choose .

sections.label: Section 1.1.99b
sections.original: Quin ruri es in praefectura tua ?
sections.translation: But why ain ’ t you in the country , at your post of command ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.100
sections.original: quin potius quod legatum est tibi negotium ,
sections.translation: Why don ’ t you rather pay attention to the business that has been entrusted to you , and keep yourself from meddling in city matters ? Have you come hither to deprive me of my betrothed ? Be off to the country — be off to your place of command , and be hanged to you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.104
sections.original: Chaline , non sum oblitus officium meum :
sections.translation: Chalinus , I have not forgotten my duty .

sections.label: Section 1.1.105
sections.original: praefeci ruri recte qui curet tamen .
sections.translation: I ’ ve given charge to one who will still take care that all ’ s right in the country . When I ’ ve got that for which I came hither to the city , to take her as my wife whom you are dying for — the fair and charming Casina , your fellow-servant — when I ’ ve carried her off with myself into the country as my wife ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.110
sections.original: ruri incubabo usque in praefectura mea .
sections.translation: I ’ ll then stick fast in the country , at my post of command .

sections.label: Section 1.1.111
sections.original: Tun illam ducas ? hercle me suspendio ,
sections.translation: What , you marry her ? By my faith , ’ twere better I should die by a halter , than that you should be the winner of her .

sections.label: Section 1.1.113
sections.original: Mea praedast illa : proin tu te in laqueum induas .
sections.translation: She ’ s my prize ; do you put yourself in a halter at once .

sections.label: Section 1.1.114
sections.original: Ex sterculino effosse , tua illaec praeda sit ?
sections.translation: Fellow , dug up from your own dunghill , is she to be your prize ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.115
sections.original: Scies hóc ita esse .
sections.translation: You ’ ll find that such is the fact .

sections.label: Section 1.1.115b
sections.original: Vae tibi .
sections.translation: Woe be unto you !

sections.label: Section 1.1.115c
sections.original: Quot te modis ,
sections.translation: in what a many ways , if I only live , I ’ ll have you tormented at my wedding !

sections.label: Section 1.1.117
sections.original: Quid tu mihi facies ?
sections.translation: What will you do to me ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.117b
sections.original: Egone quid faciam tibi ?
sections.translation: What will I do to you ? In the first place of all , you shall hold the lighted torch for this new-made bride of nine ; that always , in future , you may be worthless [*] , and not esteemed .

sections.label: Section 1.1.120
sections.original: post id locorum quando ad villam veneris ,
sections.translation: Then next after that , when you get to the country-house , a single pitcher [*] shall be found you , and a single path , a single spring , a single brass cauldron , and eight casks ; and unless these shall be always kept filled , I ’ ll load you with lashes . I ’ ll make you so thoroughly bent with carrying water ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.125
sections.original: ut postilena possit ex te fieri .
sections.translation: that a horse ’ s crupper might be manufactured out of you . And then , in future , unless in the country you either feed on pulse , or , like a worm , upon the soil , should you require to taste of any better food , never , upon my faith , is hunger as full of hungriness as I ’ ll make you to be in the country .

sections.label: Section 1.1.130
sections.original: post id , quom lassus fueris et famelicus ,
sections.translation: After that , when you ’ re tired out , and starved with famine , care shall be taken that , at night , you go to bed as you deserve .

sections.label: Section 1.1.132
sections.original: Quid facies ?
sections.translation: What will you do ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.132b
sections.original: Concludere in fénestram firmiter ,
sections.translation: You shall be shut up fast in a nook with bars , where you can listen while I ’ m caressing her , while she is saying to me , My soul , my own Olympio ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.135
sections.original: mea vita , mea mellilla , mea festivitas ,
sections.translation: my life , my sweet , my delight , do let me kiss your dear eyes , my love ! do , there ’ s a dear , let yourself be loved ! my own day of happiness , my sparrow-chick , my own dove , my leveret ! When these expressions shall be being uttered to me , then will you , you villain ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.140
sections.original: quasi mus , in medio pariete versabere .
sections.translation: be wriggling about like a mouse in the middle of the wall . Now , that you mayn ’ t be trying to give me an answer , I ’ ll off in-doors ; I ’ m tired of your talk .

sections.label: Section 1.1.142b
sections.original: Te sequor .
sections.translation: I ’ ll follow you . Here , indeed , on my word , assuredly you shall do nothing without me for an overlooker .

sections.label: Section 2.1.144
sections.original: Obsígnate céllas , reférte anulum ád me :
sections.translation: Seal fast the store-rooms [*] , bring back the signet to me .

sections.label: Section 2.1.145
sections.original: ego húc transeo ín proxumum ád meam vicínam .
sections.translation: I ’ m going here to my next door neighbour ; if my husband wants me for anything , take care and send for me thence .

sections.label: Section 2.1.147
sections.original: Prándium iússerat sénex sibi parari .
sections.translation: The old gentleman ordered a breakfast to be got ready for him to-day .

sections.label: Section 2.1.148
sections.original: St ! tace átque abi ; néque paro néque hodie coquetur ,
sections.translation: Tut ! Hold your tongue , and be off . ( PARDALISCA goes into the house .) I don ’ t prepare it , and it shan ’ t be cooked ; since he sets himself against myself and his son ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.150
sections.original: ánimi amorísque causá sui ,
sections.translation: for the sake of his passion and his appetite . A disgraceful fellow that ! I ’ ll punish this lover with hunger , I ’ ll punish him with thirst , with abuse , with hardships .

sections.label: Section 2.1.153_155
sections.original: ego pól illum probe íncommodís dictis ángam ,
sections.translation: By my faith , I ’ ll thoroughly worry him with disagreable speeches ; I ’ ll make him to pass a life in future just as he deserves — fit food for Acheron , a hunter after iniquity , a stable of infamy !

sections.label: Section 2.1.160
sections.original: nunc húc meas fortúnas eo quéstum ad vicínam .
sections.translation: Now I ’ ll away hence to my neighbours , to lament my lot . But the door makes a noise ; and see , she ’ s coming out of doors herself . On my word , I ’ ve not started for my call at a convenient time .

sections.label: Section 2.2.163_165
sections.original: Sequimíni , comites , in próxumum me huc . heus vós , ecquis haec quae lóquor audit ?
sections.translation: Follow me , my attendants [*] , here next door . You there ! Does any one hear this that I say ? I shall be here , if my husband or any person shall seek me . Did I order my distaff to be taken there ? For when I ’ m at home alone , drowsiness takes effect upon [*] my hand .

sections.label: Section 2.2.168_170b
sections.original: Myrrhína , salve .
sections.translation: Myrrhina , good morrow .

sections.label: Section 2.2.171
sections.original: Salvé mecastor . séd quid tu es tristís , amabo ?
sections.translation: Good morrow , my dear Cleostrata . But , prithee , why are you sad ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.172_175
sections.original: Itá solent omnes quae súnt male nuptae :
sections.translation: So all are wont to be who are unfortunately married ; at home and abroad , there ’ s always enough to make them sad . But I was going to your house .

sections.label: Section 2.2.177b
sections.original: Et pol ego ístuc ad te .
sections.translation: And , troth , I was coming here to yours . But what is it that now distresses your mind ? For the thing that distresses you , that same is a trouble to me .

sections.label: Section 2.2.181
sections.original: Credo écastor , nam vícinam nemínem amo merito mágis quam te
sections.translation: On my word , I do believe you . For with good reason no female neighbour of mine do I love better than yourself , nor any one with whom I have more ties of intimacy , to afford me pleasure .

sections.label: Section 2.2.183_185
sections.original: Amó te , atque istúc expetó scire quíd sit .
sections.translation: I thank you kindly , and I long to know what this is .

sections.label: Section 2.2.186
sections.original: Péssumis mé modis déspicatúr domi .
sections.translation: My husband has put slights upon me in a most unworthy manner .
